description:
You are a security-operations (SecOps) log analyst who has just been handed a single Apache style access log that already exists on the system.

  1. Location of the raw log  
     • Full path: /home/user/logs/webapp_access.log  
     • Format: the classical “combined” Apache access-log format, one request per line.  
       Example pattern (spacing may vary):  
       127.0.0.1 - - [10/Apr/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024 "-" "Mozilla/5.0"

  2. Investigation goals  
     Using *only* standard GNU/Linux CLI tooling – with the primary text-processing work being done via **awk** and **sed** – complete all of the following, step by step.  
     a. Build an aggregated summary of HTTP status codes that occur in the log.  
     b. Determine the three IP addresses that generated the highest number of requests.  
     c. Isolate every request in the log that returned an HTTP 500 status (exactly “500”, not 50x wild-cards).  
     d. Store the results in the analysis directory described below.

  3. Where to place your results  
     • Create the directory /home/user/analysis if it does not already exist.  
     • When you are finished there must be **exactly two new files** in /home/user/analysis:  
       i.  /home/user/analysis/pattern_report.log  
       ii. /home/user/analysis/error_traces.txt

  4. Very strict output specification – pattern_report.log  
     The automated tests will parse this file byte-for-byte, so be precise:  
     • UTF-8 encoded plain text.  
     • Lines are separated by the single UNIX newline character “\n”.  
     • No leading or trailing blank lines (aside from the required blank separator described below).  
     • The file must consist of **nine lines** in the exact order shown here; do not add, remove, or reorder anything.  
       Line-1 : literal header  ->  StatusCode,Requests  
       Line-2 : highest-count status code line  
       Line-3 : second highest-count status code line  
       Line-4 : third highest-count status code line  
       Line-5 : *a completely empty line* (just “\n”) acting as a visual separator  
       Line-6 : literal header  ->  TopIPs,Requests  
       Line-7 : IP with the most requests  
       Line-8 : IP with the second most requests  
       Line-9 : IP with the third most requests  
     • Data lines (Lines 2-4 and 7-9) must use a comma as the only delimiter, with no spaces, following this schema:  
       <Field-value-1>,<Field-value-2>  
       where:  
       – Field-value-1 is the status code (e.g. 404) **or** IP address (e.g. 192.168.1.10) exactly as seen in the log.  
       – Field-value-2 is the integer request count for that code or IP.  
     • Both the status-code block (Lines 2-4) and the Top-IP block (Lines 7-9) must be sorted in **descending numeric order on the Requests column**.  
       In the event of an identical count, keep the natural *lexicographic* order of the code or IP.

  5. Very strict output specification – error_traces.txt  
     • UTF-8 encoded plain text.  
     • Contains *only* the raw log lines in which the HTTP status field is exactly “500”.  
     • Preserve each matching line verbatim, including its internal spacing, quotes and timestamp.  
     • Preserve the original ordering taken from webapp_access.log.  
     • Each line ends with exactly one “\n”. No extra blank lines before or after.

  6. Hints / constraints you must obey  
     • Use awk to perform all counting, field extraction and numeric sorting.  
     • Use sed for lightweight line editing tasks, such as removing unwanted columns or inserting the mandatory blank separator.  
     • Do not move, rename or modify the original log file – it is considered evidence.  
     • Do not use third-party utilities that are not part of a standard Linux install.  
     • Make sure your commands are idempotent: running them twice should always reproduce the same pattern_report.log and error_traces.txt.  
     • The current user does not have root access; place all output strictly under /home/user.  
     • Feel free to echo helpful console output while you work; only the two exact files described above will be graded.

  Once you are happy with your results, list the two files with their sizes to confirm creation.
